title: Classes vs Structs 🏛️
description: Understand the massive difference between Value and Reference types.

slides.title: The Twin Brother 🏛️
slides.content:
# Enter the Class

We just learned how Structs allow us to bundle custom data together. Swift has a second way to do this: the **Class**.

A Class (`class`) looks almost identical to a `struct`. It has Properties. It has Methods. You use dot notation to call them.

So why does Swift have both? The difference lies in **how they copy data**.

slides.title: Value Types (Structs) 📄
slides.content:
# The Photocopy

Structs are **Value Types**. When you assign a Struct to a new variable, Swift makes a 100% full photocopy of the data.

```swift
struct Score { var points = 10 }
var a = Score()
var b = a // b is a brand new PHOTOCOPY

b.points = 20
print(a.points) // Prints 10. The original was safe!
```
Changing `b` did absolutely nothing to `a`.

slides.title: Reference Types (Classes) 🔗
slides.content:
# Sharing the Document

Classes are **Reference Types**. They do NOT photocopy. When you assign a Class to an new variable, you are creating a shortcut pointing to the EXACT SAME block of data!

```swift
class Score { var points = 10 }
var a = Score()
var b = a // b points to the SAME EXACT DATA as a

b.points = 20
print(a.points) // Prints 20!!!
```
Because `b` and `a` share the same data, changing one changes BOTH.

quizData.question: If you want to ensure that every time you pass your custom object it is safely CLONED without affecting the original, should you use a Struct or a Class?
quizData.explanation: Structs are Value Types. They always duplicate/clone themselves when passed around, ensuring total safety.

quizData.question: If you want multiple screens in your app to point to one Single 'Database' object, so that when Screen 2 updates the data, Screen 1 instantly sees the update... should you use a Struct or a Class?
quizData.explanation: Classes are Reference Types. Because they share memory, modifying the Class in one place instantly updates it everywhere else!

slides.title: See It in Action
slides.content: Run this code and prove to yourself the massive difference between referencing shared data vs cloning it!
